invariant_definition: *Rasa* is not merely "flavor." It is the technical savoring of a stabilized affective configuration. In poetics, the classic formula is that *rasa* arises from the conjunction of *vibhāva*, *anubhāva*, and *vyabhicāri-bhāva* in relation to a *sthāyi-bhāva*. Abhinavagupta's crucial move is to explain how such savoring becomes universalized rather than merely private, and why its relish is structurally akin to contemplative bliss. Later Vedāntic and devotional authors redeploy this aesthetic apparatus rather than abandoning it. Madhusūdana reads devotion through *rasa*; Gauḍīya theologians make *rasa* constitutive of the eternal relation between *jīva* and Bhagavān. The term therefore carries an entire theoretical scaffold: cause, expression, stable affect, relish, universalization, and the ontological status of the enjoyed object. In devotional use especially, Bhagavān is not a symbol used for aesthetic transport but the real object around which the affective world is organized.

per_school.definition: *Rasa* in Madhusūdana's Advaita is the relishable form taken by *bhakti-yoga* toward Mukunda, and it may appear either conjoined with the nine aesthetic *rasas* or in its own unsublated purity as a supreme *puruṣārtha* [[BR 1.1](cite://madhusudana/bhakti-rasayana/1.1)]. Bhakti is not here reduced to *brahma-vidyā*; by the *saṃyoga-pṛthaktva-nyāya* the two are assigned distinct *phala-sādhana-bhāvas* even where preparatory conditions overlap [[BR 1.1 ṭīkā.10](cite://madhusudana/bhakti-rasayana/1.1 ṭīkā.10)]. Its savor is moreover *laukika-rasa-vilakṣaṇa*: the devotee once seized by it does not wish to relinquish Mukunda, and its bliss is said to remain untouched by the sorrow that overtakes worldly relish [[BR 1.1 ṭīkā.14](cite://madhusudana/bhakti-rasayana/1.1 ṭīkā.14); [BR 1.1 ṭīkā.16](cite://madhusudana/bhakti-rasayana/1.1 ṭīkā.16)].

per_school.not_applicable_reason: The on-disk Rāmānuja corpus treats bhakti, upāsanā, and kaiṅkarya extensively, but it does not present a distinct Viśiṣṭādvaitin technical doctrine of rasa.

The available Madhva, Jayatīrtha-, Vyāsatīrtha-, and Rāghavendra-linked materials on disk do not deploy rasa as a distinct Tattva-vādin technical category rather than as ordinary vocabulary for devotion or sensible taste.
The surviving on-disk Bhāskara and Yādava-Prakāśa materials are ontological reconstructions and do not witness a distinct Bhedābheda doctrine of rasa.

per_school.definition: Rūpa Gosvāmī presents the whole treatise as an "ocean of the nectar of the *rasa* of devotion to Bhagavān," so *rasa* is not ancillary ornament but the architectonic form in which Gauḍīya theology orders devotional life [[BRS 1.1.7](cite://rupa-gosvami/bhakti-rasamrta-sindhu/1.1.7)]. The qualifying condition is *uttamā-bhakti*: favorable *kṛṣṇānuśīlana* free from extraneous desire and from subordination to *jñāna* and *karma*, which gives the theological subject-matter that *rasa* will later articulate [[BRS 1.1.11](cite://rupa-gosvami/bhakti-rasamrta-sindhu/1.1.11)]. The purity of devotion is therefore internal to the definition of the school's *bhakti-rasa*, not a later embellishment added after the fact [[BRS 1.1.16](cite://rupa-gosvami/bhakti-rasamrta-sindhu/1.1.16)].

In Nimbārka's synopsis, *bhakti-rasa* belongs to a fivefold doctrinal map that begins from the *upāsya* and the *upāsaka*, proceeds through *kṛpā-phala*, and culminates in a savor beyond that prior grace-mediated attainment [[DaŚ 10](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/10)]. The *upāsya* is not indeterminate Brahman but Kṛṣṇa together with Vṛṣabhānu's daughter, so the rasa under discussion is already personally and relationally specified [[DaŚ 4](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/4); [DaŚ 5](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/5)]. *Rasa* here therefore names the consummation of devotional relation, not an impersonal affect detached from the *upāsya-upāsaka-bhāva* [[DaŚ 10](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/10)].

*Rasa* in Sāṃkhya is a sensible object within the bhogya-side of prakṛti rather than an autonomous aesthetic absolute: the system counts *rasana* among the eleven indriyas and thereby fixes taste as one determinate domain of experience [[SK 26](cite://isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/26)]. The commentarial tradition makes the point explicit by invoking the six tastes as evidence that enjoyed objects require a distinct *bhoktṛ* over against the aggregate of the enjoyed [[SK 17 comm.](cite://isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/17-commentary)]. At the level of emanation, *rasa-tanmātra* is the subtle determinant from which water emerges in the standard series of bhūta-production [[SK 22 comm.](cite://isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika/22-commentary)].

In Yoga, *rasa* enters the praxis of concentration as *rasasaṃvit*, a yogically induced taste-cognition that steadies the citta, removes doubt, and opens onto samādhi-prajñā [[YBh 1.35](cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/1.35-bhasya)]. The same ontology classifies *rasa-tanmātra* as the subtle object corresponding to water, so taste belongs to the graded sequence from gross sensory support to subtle viṣaya traversed by yogic samāpatti [[YBh 1.45](cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/1.45-bhasya)].

In Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika, *rasa* is a real *guṇa* and an irreducible viṣaya-domain, not a merely derivative aspect of some wider sensible whole [[VS 1.1.5](cite://kanada/vaisesika-sutra/1.1.5); [NS 3.1.56-62](cite://aksapada/nyaya-sutra/3.1.56-62)]. Vaiśeṣika assigns taste to earth and water within its dravya-guṇa analysis, while Nyāya correlates that objective domain with a distinct *rasana* among the indriyas [[VS 2.1.1-2](cite://kanada/vaisesika-sutra/2.1.1-2); [NS 1.1.12](cite://aksapada/nyaya-sutra/1.1.12)].

In Pūrva-Mīmāṃsā, *rasa* is not an aesthetic endpoint but a determinate sensory object deployed in pramāṇa-theory and ritual exegesis [[ŚV comm. line 3846](cite://kumarila/sloka-varttika/commentary-line-3846); [ŚBh 4.1.23](cite://shabara/mimamsa-sutra-bhasya/4.1.23)]. Kumārila treats cognitions of taste as part of the class of cases for which the self-mind conjunction is too generic to explain a specific perceptual episode, so the relevant indriya must be fixed through its *asādhāraṇa* causal role [[ŚV comm. line 3846](cite://kumarila/sloka-varttika/commentary-line-3846)]. Śabara, in turn, uses perceptible savor as an inferential marker in deciding ritual classification, distinguishing *āmikṣā* from *vājina* through the taste that reveals the milk-curd mixture [[ŚBh 4.1.23](cite://shabara/mimamsa-sutra-bhasya/4.1.23)].

per_school.not_applicable_reason: The on-disk Mādhyamaka sources mention rasana only incidentally in standard lists of faculties and do not develop a specifically Mādhyamika doctrine of rasa itself.

per_school.definition: In Dharmakīrti's epistemological Yogācāra, *rasa* functions as a momentary sensible effect within a causal complex rather than as a persisting substrate-quality [[PV 3.9-10](cite://dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/3.9-10)]. One may move from tasted quality to an unseen co-effect such as form because both depend on one causal assemblage; the inferential force lies in causal efficacy and common production, not in the endurance of a single substance bearing multiple properties [[PV 3.9-10](cite://dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/3.9-10)].

In Pratyabhijñā / Trika, *rasa* is refunctioned as the savor of consciousness itself: Kṣemarāja speaks of manifestation as an expansion marked by *cid-rasa-śyānatā*, the saturation of tattva, bhuvana, and pramātṛ series by consciousness' own plenitude [[PH 4 vṛtti](cite://ksemaraja/pratyabhijna-hrdayam/4-vrtti)]. In the yogic register, this same logic becomes practical instruction: the yogin is to soak waking and the intervening states with *turya*-bliss-rasa and enter through the inward *camatkāra-rasa* of reflective absorption [[ŚSV 3.20.6](cite://ksemaraja/shiva-sutra-vimarshini/3.20.6); [ŚSV 3.21.3-4](cite://ksemaraja/shiva-sutra-vimarshini/3.21.3-4)].

translator_note: 'Flavor' and 'taste' are etymologically helpful but philosophically thin. They can suggest a merely subjective preference. *Rasa* names a technical aesthetics of relishable affect, and in devotional traditions it imports a theology in which the enjoyed object is real and personally specified.
